sentences_input.text: My relationship with my father-- obviously, it can't be given in a straight definition, simply because I lived with him right up to the age of 20.

And the relationship of children and parents, and teenagers and parents, and then grown up and parents-- especially when the-- the-- my generation compared with his generation had made such strides in terms of attitudes toward so many things, and to life in general, and to Jewish life in particular.
Obviously, it had its points of-- of disagreement, and points of argument, and so on, which-- part of the spice of life, as far as my personal life is concerned-- [COUGHS] excuse me.

In the-- he sent me to different primary [NON-ENGLISH], a sort of a full-time day schools-- primary day schools, which in those days meant from 9:00 in the morning till 4:00 or 5:00 in the afternoon at least.

And I had one school where I lived-- where I stayed till 7:00 in the evening, when-- in the winter time, in the Bialystok when it was dark, we used to walk home with lanterns in our hands, not so much really for the need of it, but for the quaintness of it, for children.

I remember making out of a tin of [? spreads, ?] making holes in it and put a candle in it, because batteries and these sort of things was-- if they were available, they weren't available to everybody.
And so just to give an idea of the time [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] span, in retrospect.
